lessons.title: Musculoskeletal Root Words and Key Terms
objectives: Identify root words for bone, muscle, cartilage, and joint structures
Decode common musculoskeletal conditions, procedures, and assessment terms
Distinguish between terms for inflammation, disease, and structural changes of the musculoskeletal system
Use musculoskeletal terminology accurately in documentation

conceptBlocks.heading: Bone and Joint Root Words
conceptBlocks.body:
oste/o = bone (osteoporosis, osteomyelitis, osteoblast)
arth/r/o = joint (arthritis, arthroscopy, arthroplasty)
chondr/o = cartilage (chondritis, chondromalacia)
costal/o = rib (intercostal = between ribs)
vertebr/o / spondyl/o = vertebra (vertebral, spondylosis)
myeloma = tumor of bone marrow (myel/o = bone marrow/spinal cord — context-dependent!)
cranio = skull (craniotomy, cranioplasty)
synovi/o = synovial membrane lining joints (synovitis, synovial fluid)

conceptBlocks.heading: Muscle Root Words
conceptBlocks.body:
my/o / muscul/o = muscle (myalgia, myopathy, muscular dystrophy)
tend/o / tendin/o = tendon (tendinitis, tendon rupture)
fasci/o = fascia (fasciitis — as in plantar fasciitis)
ligament/o = ligament (ligamentous laxity)

Key conditions:
myalgia = muscle pain (-algia = pain)
myositis = inflammation of muscle (-itis)
myopathy = disease of muscle (-pathy)
rhabdomyolysis = breakdown of muscle tissue (rhabdo = striated muscle; lysis = destruction)

conceptBlocks.heading: Common Musculoskeletal Conditions
conceptBlocks.body:
osteoporosis = decreased bone density (osteo + por/o = pore + -osis = condition)
osteoarthritis = degenerative joint disease (bone + joint + inflammation)
rheumatoid arthritis = autoimmune joint inflammation (rheumato = flowing; immune-mediated)
fracture = break in a bone (not a word-part term but clinically essential)
dislocation = joint bones displaced out of alignment
subluxation = partial dislocation (sub- = partial; lux = to displace)
tendinitis / tendinopathy = tendon inflammation or degeneration

lessons.teachingNotes: Note that myel/o is context-dependent — in neurology it refers to the spinal cord; in hematology/oncology it refers to bone marrow. Students must use context (what system is being discussed?) to determine meaning. This is a good critical-thinking teaching moment.

lessons.title: Neurological Root Words and Common Terms
objectives: Identify root words for brain, spinal cord, nerve, and associated neurological structures
Decode common neurological conditions, procedures, and assessment terms
Distinguish between motor, sensory, and structural neurological terms
Recognize high-alert neurological terms in clinical documentation

conceptBlocks.heading: Neurological Structure Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
neur/o = nerve (neurology, neuropathy, neuritis)
encephal/o = brain (encephalitis, encephalopathy, electroencephalogram = EEG)
cerebell/o = cerebellum (cerebellar ataxia)
cerebr/o = cerebrum / brain (cerebrovascular, cerebral palsy)
myel/o = spinal cord (myelopathy, myelogram — in neuro context)
meningo = meninges (meningitis, meningocele)
radicul/o = nerve root (radiculopathy)
gangli/o = ganglion (ganglionitis)

conceptBlocks.heading: Neurological Function Terms
conceptBlocks.body:
Motor system terms:
-plegia = paralysis (hemiplegia, quadriplegia, paraplegia)
-paresis = partial weakness (hemiparesis = weakness on one side)
-kinesia = movement (bradykinesia = slow movement; dyskinesia = impaired movement)

Sensory system terms:
-esthesia = sensation (anesthesia = without sensation; paresthesia = abnormal sensation such as tingling)
neuralgia = nerve pain
hyperalgia = increased pain sensitivity

Mind/cognition terms:
-phasia = speech (aphasia = without speech; dysphasia = impaired speech)
-praxia = purposeful movement (apraxia = inability to perform learned movements)

conceptBlocks.heading: Common Neurological Conditions and Procedures
conceptBlocks.body:
meningitis = inflammation of the meninges (brain/spinal cord coverings)
encephalitis = inflammation of brain tissue
myelopathy = disease of the spinal cord
neuropathy = disease of peripheral nerves
cerebrovascular accident (CVA) = stroke (disruption of blood flow to the brain)
lumbar puncture (LP) = spinal tap to obtain cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
EEG (electroencephalogram) = recording of brain electrical activity
MRI of the brain = magnetic resonance imaging — standard for CNS evaluation
craniotomy = surgical opening of the skull (cranio + -otomy)

lessons.teachingNotes: The plegia vs paresis distinction is high-yield: plegia = complete paralysis; paresis = partial/incomplete weakness. Also emphasize aphasia (no speech) vs dysphasia (impaired speech) — this distinction matters when documenting post-stroke communication deficits. CVA (cerebrovascular accident) is the formal term for stroke and appears throughout clinical documentation.

questions.stem: The suffix '-plegia' in the term 'hemiplegia' refers to:
questions.rationale: -Plegia = complete paralysis. Hemiplegia (hemi- = half/one side) = complete paralysis of one side of the body. -Paresis = partial weakness (hemiparesis = one-sided weakness). -Esthesia = sensation. -Pathy = disease (-myopathy).

questions.stem: The root word 'oste/o' refers to:
questions.rationale: Oste/o = bone. Osteoporosis = porous (decreased density) bone condition. Osteoarthritis = degenerative disease of bone and joints. My/o = muscle. Chondr/o = cartilage. Arth/r/o = joint.

questions.stem: A patient is diagnosed with 'aphasia' after a stroke. This means the patient:

options.B: Is unable to speak or has severely impaired language
options.C: Has weakness on one side of the body
options.D: Is experiencing tingling sensations in the extremities

questions.rationale: A- = without; -phasia = speech/language. Aphasia = loss of ability to speak or understand language. Blindness = amaurosis or visual field defect. Hemiplegia = one-sided paralysis. Paresthesia = abnormal sensations like tingling (par- = abnormal; -esthesia = sensation).

questions.stem: The term 'meningitis' refers to inflammation of the:

options.C: Membranes covering the brain and spinal cord

questions.rationale: Meningo = meninges (the three-layered membranes covering the brain and spinal cord); -itis = inflammation. Meningitis = inflammation of the meninges. Encephalitis = brain tissue inflammation. Myelitis = spinal cord inflammation. Neuritis or neuropathy = peripheral nerve disease.

questions.stem: A nursing note states the patient has 'paresthesia in the right hand.' This symptom is best described as:

options.C: Abnormal sensations such as tingling or numbness

questions.rationale: Par- = abnormal, beside; -esthesia = sensation. Paresthesia = abnormal sensation, typically described as tingling, 'pins and needles,' numbness, or burning. Anesthesia (an- = without) = complete loss of sensation. Myospasm = muscle cramps. Paresis = weakness.

questions.stem: In neurological terminology, 'myel/o' specifically refers to the spinal cord when used in which context?

options.B: When describing conditions of the nervous system such as myelopathy
options.C: When the term is used in hematology lab reports

questions.rationale: Myel/o is context-dependent: in neurology, it refers to the spinal cord (myelopathy = spinal cord disease; myelogram = imaging of the spinal cord). In hematology/oncology, myel/o refers to bone marrow (myeloma = bone marrow tumor). Always use the clinical context to determine which meaning applies.
questions.keyConceptTested: myel/o context-dependency — spinal cord vs bone marrow

questions.stem: Arthroscopy is a procedure that involves:

options.C: Visual examination of the inside of a joint using a small camera

questions.rationale: Arth/r/o = joint; -scopy = visual examination using a scope. Arthroscopy = direct visualization inside a joint (commonly the knee) using a small camera inserted through a small incision. Arthrectomy = joint removal. Arthrography = X-ray of joint. Arthroplasty (-plasty) = surgical reconstruction or joint replacement.

questions.stem: The term 'osteoporosis' is best decoded as:

options.B: Abnormal condition of porous, decreased-density bone
options.C: Bone tumor or malignant bone growth
options.D: Infection of the bone and bone marrow

questions.rationale: Oste/o = bone; por/o = pore (porous); -osis = abnormal condition. Osteoporosis = condition of abnormally porous bone with decreased mineral density, increasing fracture risk. Osteitis = bone inflammation. Osteosarcoma = bone tumor. Osteomyelitis = infection of bone and marrow (myel/o in bone context).

questions.stem: A patient's chart notes 'hemiparesis of the left side.' This means the patient has:

options.A: Complete paralysis of the left side of the body
options.B: Partial weakness on the left side of the body
options.C: Tingling and numbness on the left side
options.D: Loss of sensation on the left side

questions.rationale: Hemi- = half (one side); -paresis = partial or incomplete weakness. Hemiparesis = partial weakness affecting one side of the body. Hemiplegia (-plegia) = complete paralysis of one side. Paresthesia = abnormal sensations. Anesthesia = loss of sensation.

questions.stem: The root word 'arth/r/o' in medical terminology refers to:
questions.rationale: Arth/r/o = joint. Arthritis = joint inflammation. Arthroplasty = joint replacement surgery. My/o = muscle. Oste/o = bone. Tend/o / tendin/o = tendon.

questions.stem: A cerebrovascular accident (CVA) is the formal medical term for a:
questions.rationale: Cerebrovascular = pertaining to the cerebral blood vessels (cerebr/o = brain; vascular = blood vessels); accident = sudden unplanned event. A CVA (stroke) is the sudden disruption of blood supply to part of the brain, causing neurological deficits. Myocardial infarction = heart attack. Neoplasm = tumor. Seizure uses different terminology.

questions.stem: Which term describes a surgical procedure involving an opening into the skull?
questions.rationale: Cranio = skull; -otomy = cutting into. Craniotomy = surgical incision into the skull, typically to access the brain. Cranioplasty (-plasty) = surgical repair/reconstruction of the skull. Encephaloscopy would mean visual examination of the brain. Meningectomy = removal of the meninges.

questions.stem: The medical term for muscle pain is:
questions.rationale: My/o = muscle; -algia = pain. Myalgia = muscle pain. Myopathy (-pathy) = muscle disease. Myositis (-itis) = muscle inflammation. Myomalacia = softening of muscle tissue (-malacia = softening).

questions.stem: Which of the following is the correct suffix for a condition meaning 'disease of' a structure?
questions.rationale: -Pathy means disease of. Neuropathy = disease of nerves. Cardiomyopathy = disease of heart muscle. -Itis = inflammation. -Osis = abnormal condition or process. -Ectomy = surgical removal.

questions.stem: The term 'arthroplasty' refers to:

options.C: Surgical repair or reconstruction of a joint

questions.rationale: Arth/r/o = joint; -plasty = surgical repair or reconstruction. Arthroplasty includes joint replacement (e.g., total knee arthroplasty = TKA; total hip arthroplasty = THA). Arthrectomy = joint removal. Arthritis = inflammation. Arthroscopy = visual examination.

questions.stem: Which term is correctly defined as 'inflammation of the nerves'?
questions.rationale: Neur/o = nerve; -itis = inflammation. Neuritis = inflammation of a nerve or nerves. Neuropathy (-pathy) = disease of nerves. Neuralgia (-algia) = nerve pain. Neurosis = an older psychiatric term not specifically about nerve inflammation.

questions.stem: A nurse reads that a patient has 'bradykinesia' on their neurology assessment. This finding describes:
questions.rationale: Brady- = slow; -kinesia = movement. Bradykinesia = abnormally slow movement, a characteristic feature of Parkinson's disease. Tachykinesia = rapid movement. Akinesia (a- = without) = complete absence of movement. Ataxia or dyskinesia = incoordination.

questions.stem: Which root word is specifically associated with cartilage?
questions.rationale: Chondr/o = cartilage. Chondritis = cartilage inflammation. Chondromalacia = cartilage softening (e.g., chondromalacia patellae). Oste/o = bone. Synovi/o = synovial membrane. Tend/o = tendon.

questions.stem: The term 'encephalopathy' is best defined as:

options.B: Disease or dysfunction of the brain

questions.rationale: Encephal/o = brain; -pathy = disease/dysfunction. Encephalopathy = any disease or dysfunction of the brain (e.g., hepatic encephalopathy from liver failure; Wernicke's encephalopathy from thiamine deficiency). Encephalitis (-itis) = inflammation. Craniotomy = surgical incision. Glioma/meningioma = brain tumors.

questions.stem: A patient is described as having 'quadriplegia.' This means the patient has:

options.B: Paralysis of one side of the body
options.D: Paralysis of only the lower body

questions.rationale: Quadri- = four; -plegia = complete paralysis. Quadriplegia = paralysis of all four limbs, typically from cervical spinal cord injury. Hemiplegia (hemi- = half) = one-sided paralysis. Paraplegia (para- = beside/both legs) = lower body paralysis. -Paresis = partial weakness.

questions.stem: Which of the following terms correctly uses a root word to refer to a tendon?
questions.rationale: Tendin/o (also tend/o) = tendon. Tendinitis = inflammation of a tendon. Myalgia = muscle pain (my/o = muscle). Arthropathy = joint disease (arth/r/o = joint). Chondrectomy = surgical removal of cartilage (chondr/o = cartilage).

questions.stem: A lumbar puncture (spinal tap) is performed to obtain cerebrospinal fluid. The root 'cerebro-spinal' refers to the:
questions.rationale: Cerebro- = cerebrum/brain; spinal = pertaining to the spine/spinal cord. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) bathes and cushions the brain and spinal cord. A lumbar puncture samples this fluid from the lumbar subarachnoid space. This is not related to the heart, peripheral nerves alone, or meninges alone.
